concept_name: Time to Meet — Distance Divided by Relative Approach Speed
source_book: DC Pandey 6.10 — classic approach problems; split from relative_motion

real_world_anchor.primary: Two Rajdhani trains on the Delhi-Mumbai route — one starts from Delhi southbound at 100 km/h, the other from Mumbai northbound at 120 km/h. They are 1400 km apart at the start. Classic JEE problem: when and where do they meet? The answer is driven by one formula: time = distance divided by relative approach speed, i.e. 1400 / (100+120) = 6.36 hours. A problem every Indian student has seen.
real_world_anchor.secondary: A ranger and a stray elephant near Bandipur forest. The elephant walks toward a village at 6 km/h; the ranger sets out from the village on a bike at 30 km/h to intercept it. If they start 12 km apart, time to meet = 12 / (6+30) = 20 minutes.
real_world_anchor.tertiary: A Chandrayaan orbit-correction burn. The spacecraft is 2 km from a planned meeting point and closing at 0.5 m/s. Time to arrive = 2000 / 0.5 = 4000 seconds — about 67 minutes. Same formula; different scale.

formulas.approaching: If A and B move toward each other: time_to_meet = d / (V_A + V_B)
formulas.chasing: If A chases B (same direction, V_A > V_B): time_to_meet = d / (V_A - V_B)
formulas.same_speed_same_direction: If V_A = V_B same direction: they never meet, time = infinity.
formulas.general_form: time_to_meet = d / |relative speed of approach|. The formula is ALWAYS the same; only the relative speed changes with geometry.

constraints: Requires both objects to be moving toward the same point at some moment.
Relative speed must be the RATE at which the gap closes, not the raw sum of speeds.
Answer is in the same time units as d/V.

STATE_1.title: Closing the Gap — The Core Intuition

scene_composition.text:
The physical question:

  Gap between A and B  =  d
  Gap closes every second by  =  relative approach speed  =  v_rel

  So total time to close the gap fully:

                    d
       t_meet  =  -----
                   v_rel

  A kilometre-wide moat, drained at 10 m/s,
  empties in 100 seconds.

  That's it. Entire formula.
  The ONLY trick is getting v_rel right for each geometry.

tts_sentences.text_en: Two objects are separated by a gap. That gap closes every second at the relative approach speed.
So time to meet equals initial separation divided by relative approach speed — that is the entire formula.
A kilometre-wide moat that drains at 10 metres per second empties in 100 seconds. The ratio IS the answer.
The only subtlety is identifying the correct relative speed for each geometry — same direction, opposite direction, or at an angle.

STATE_2.title: Approaching Case — Relative Speed = V_A + V_B

scene_composition.text:
Two Rajdhani trains, opposite directions:

  Distance Delhi to Mumbai:   d  =  1400 km
  Delhi Rajdhani:              V_A  =  +100 km/h  (south)
  Mumbai Rajdhani:             V_B  =  +120 km/h  (north)

  Gap closes at:
     v_rel  =  100  +  120  =  220 km/h

  Time to meet:
     t  =  1400 / 220  =  6.36 hours  ~  6 hours 22 minutes

  Where do they meet?
     Delhi Rajdhani covers  100 * 6.36  ~  636 km south.
     That is near Ratlam -- exactly as signalling logs show!
Delhi Rajdhani vs Mumbai Rajdhani — meeting en route

tts_sentences.text_en: Now the classic approach problem — two trains coming toward each other.
Delhi Rajdhani at 100, Mumbai Rajdhani at 120, separated by 1400 kilometres.
Because they are approaching, the gap closes at 100 plus 120 — 220 kilometres per hour.
Time to meet is 1400 divided by 220 — just over 6.36 hours, or 6 hours 22 minutes.
Multiply back: Delhi Rajdhani travels 636 kilometres south in that time — placing the meeting point near Ratlam in Madhya Pradesh. Matches real railway signalling logs.

STATE_3.title: Chase Case — Relative Speed = V_A − V_B

scene_composition.text:
Chase problem (same direction):

  A police jeep chases a thief's bike on NH-48.

  Thief:   V_B  =  60 km/h  (ahead)
  Jeep:    V_A  =  80 km/h  (behind)
  Head start:  d  =  1 km

  The gap closes at:
     v_rel  =  80  -  60  =  20 km/h

  Time to catch:
     t  =  1 / 20  =  0.05 hr  =  3 minutes

  Distance jeep travels in 3 min  =  80 * 0.05  =  4 km.
  Thief travels                   =  60 * 0.05  =  3 km.
  Difference  =  1 km  <-  equals the head start.  Check!

tts_sentences.text_en: Second case — same direction, the chase problem.
A thief's bike runs ahead at 60 kilometres per hour. A police jeep gives chase at 80 from behind, starting 1 kilometre back.
Because both move the same way, the gap closes at only 80 minus 60 — that is 20 kilometres per hour.
Time to catch up is 1 divided by 20 hours — three minutes. Same formula, just a different relative speed.
Quick sanity check — the jeep covers 4 kilometres in those 3 minutes, the thief covers 3. Difference is 1 kilometre, which is exactly the head start. The numbers close, the method works.

epic_c_branches.misconception_summary: Student uses d divided by ONE of the speeds (the larger one) and ignores the relative concept entirely.
confusion_patterns: time to meet just use my velocity
only use one train speed for time to meet

scene_composition.text:
Student's guess:

  Two Rajdhanis, 1400 km apart.
  Delhi Rajdhani at 100 km/h.

  "Time = distance / speed
   = 1400 / 100
   = 14 hours"

  But wait — the OTHER train is also moving toward it.
  The total gap is being eaten from BOTH sides.

  14 hours overshoots reality by more than a factor of 2.
(scene under construction — primitives backfilled to satisfy minimum)

tts_sentences.text_en: The common mistake — use only ONE train's speed and divide.
Delhi Rajdhani at 100, distance 1400. Student writes 14 hours.
But the other train is eating into the gap too! You have ignored half the picture.
The true answer is less than half — around 6.36 hours, not 14.

STATE_2.title: Correction: Gap-Closing Speed is the SUM

scene_composition.text:
Why addition for approaching:

  Every second the Delhi Rajdhani advances 100 / 3600 km south.
  Every second the Mumbai Rajdhani advances 120 / 3600 km north.
  Both bites reduce the gap.

  Total gap-reduction per second:
     (100 + 120) / 3600 km

  So relative approach speed  =  100 + 120  =  220 km/h.

  t_meet  =  d / v_rel  =  1400 / 220  =  6.36 hrs

  The FORMULA is untouched.  What changed was the speed you plug in.
Correction: Gap-Closing Speed is the SUM
(scene under construction — primitives backfilled to satisfy minimum)

tts_sentences.text_en: Fix: use the relative approach speed, not either ground speed alone.
Both trains eat into the gap simultaneously — the gap shrinks by 100 plus 120 kilometres every hour.
Plug 1400 divided by 220 and you get 6.36 hours — the right answer.

scene_composition.text:
Rule before you compute:

  Step 1: Sketch positions and direction arrows.
  Step 2: Decide whether they APPROACH (v_rel = V_A + V_B)
          or CHASE    (v_rel = V_A - V_B).
  Step 3: Only THEN plug into t = d / v_rel.

  The formula is one line.
  The thinking is all in the geometry.

  A 30-second sketch saves a 30-minute mistake.
(scene under construction — primitives backfilled to satisfy minimum)

tts_sentences.text_en: The rule to absorb is this — identify the geometry BEFORE you plug in numbers.
Sketch the positions and direction arrows, decide approach or chase, and only then write t equals d over relative speed.
The formula is just one line. The real thinking is all in the geometry.

epic_c_branches.misconception: Belief: Use Ground Speeds Alone (auto-generated from branch_id — needs author rewrite).
